title: Cat Ear Discharge: Why Your Cat Has Yellow Pus in Their Ear
meta_desc: Learn what yellow pus in your cat's ear means. Understand urgency levels and how to help your pet find relief from painful ear infections.
content_html:
What is it?

Yellow pus or discharge draining from a cat's ear is a significant indicator of an underlying health issue, most commonly a bacterial or yeast infection. This fluid is a collection of white blood cells and debris produced by the body while fighting off an infection. It often occurs alongside inflammation of the ear canal (otitis externa) and can be triggered by ear mites, allergies, or foreign objects trapped in the ear.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While yellow discharge is rarely an immediate life-threatening crisis, it is extremely painful and uncomfortable for your cat. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. Delaying treatment can allow the infection to travel deeper into the middle or inner ear, which can lead to permanent hearing loss, ruptured eardrums, or neurological issues like a persistent head tilt.

Why a photo helps triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photograph of the ear and the discharge can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary triage team. A photo allows the professional to assess the volume, color, and consistency of the pus, as well as the degree of swelling or redness in the visible part of the ear canal. This visual information helps the clinic determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and helps them prepare the necessary diagnostic tools before you arrive.

faqs.question: Can I clean the ear at home before seeing a vet?
faqs.answer: It is generally recommended to wait. Probing the ear or using the wrong cleaning solution can push debris deeper or cause pain. Your vet will want to see the discharge in its natural state to take an accurate sample.

faqs.question: Are ear infections in cats contagious?
faqs.answer: It depends on the cause. If the infection is triggered by ear mites, it is highly contagious to other pets in the household. Bacterial or yeast infections are typically not contagious but require specific medical treatment.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of a cat ear infection?
faqs.answer: In addition to yellow pus, look for frequent head shaking, scratching at the ears, a foul odor, redness, or your cat being sensitive to touch around their head.
